title: Keweenaw Bay Indian Community Land Claim Settlement Act of 2025
short_title: Keweenaw Bay Indian Community Land Claim Settlement Act of 2025
stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Indian and Insular Affairs.
plain_summary: This bill gives $33.9 million to the Keweenaw Bay Indian Community in Michigan. The money pays them back for land that the federal government took from their reservation without paying for it. In return, the tribe agrees to give up all legal claims to those lands. Current landowners will get clear ownership of their property. The tribe can use the money for anything legal except buying land for casinos.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Keweenaw Bay Indian Community Land Claim Settlement Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has found these facts to be true. The Keweenaw Bay Indian Community is an official tribe that the federal government recognizes. They live on the L'Anse Indian Reservation in Baraga County in the Upper Peninsula of Michigan. The Community has legal rights that come from two old treaties with the United States government. The first treaty was signed in eighteen forty two. This treaty gave the Community the right to use a large area of land until the government properly took away that right. The second treaty was signed in eighteen fifty four. This treaty created the L'Anse Indian Reservation as a permanent place for the Community to live. The treaty became official law when the President and Senate approved it on January tenth eighteen fifty five. In eighteen fifty Congress passed the Swamp Land Act. This law let the state of Arkansas and other states including Michigan claim swamp lands that were not good for farming. The government had not sold these lands yet. After this law passed Michigan claimed thousands of acres of swamp land. Between eighteen ninety three and nineteen thirty seven the General Land Office gave Michigan two thousand seven hundred forty three acres of land inside the reservation boundaries. These are called the Reservation Swamp Lands. The Community still had the right to use this land when the United States gave it to the state. In eighteen fifty two Congress passed the Canal Land Act to help build the Sault Ste Marie Canal. This canal would connect Lake Superior to Lake Huron. Under this law the United States let Michigan choose seven hundred fifty thousand acres of unsold public land in the state to pay for building the canal. Michigan chose at least one thousand three hundred thirty three and up to two thousand seven hundred twenty acres inside the reservation boundaries. These are called the Reservation Canal Lands. The Department of the Interior approved Michigan's land choices including the Reservation Canal Lands after the eighteen fifty four treaty was approved. The Secretary noted this approval was subject to any valid rights that already existed. The eighteen fifty four treaty set apart all unsold land within the reservation for the Community as of September thirtieth eighteen fifty four. This date came before Michigan got legal title to the Reservation Canal Lands. The Community made claims to the Department of the Interior about the Reservation Swamp Lands and the Reservation Canal Lands. They provided legal analysis and historical evidence to support their claims. In December two thousand twenty one the Department of the Interior said they carefully reviewed all documents including the tribe's expert reports. They determined that the tribe's claims to the Swamp Lands and Canal Lands have merit. The United States through the General Land Office took away the Community's exclusive right to use and live on the Reservation Swamp Lands and the Reservation Canal Lands within the reservation. The government did this without fair payment as required by the Fifth Amendment to the Constitution. The loss of the Reservation Swamp Lands and the Reservation Canal Lands without fair payment has harmed the Community in several ways. It has affected their ability to practice their culture religion and get food from the land. It has caused a harmful separation between the Community and their land. It has hurt their ability to fully develop their economy within the reservation. It has had a negative economic impact on the Community's development. Some non Indian people companies and local governments now live on land within the reservation boundaries. They bought the Reservation Swamp Lands and the Reservation Canal Lands in good faith believing they had clear ownership. They have an interest in having clear title to that land. This law allows the United States to create a fair settlement for past wrongs done to the Community when the United States took the Reservation Swamp Lands and the Reservation Canal Lands without fair payment. It also protects the ownership of non Indian people who live on the Reservation Swamp Lands and the Reservation Canal Lands by settling the Community's claims to that land and clearing up any questions about who owns the land. A settlement will allow the Community to receive fair payment and local landowners to get clear title to their land. This avoids long expensive court cases that would be costly and harmful to everyone involved. This law achieves both justice for the Community and security for current landowners through a healing process that does not create conflict.

sections.title: Section 3 Purposes
sections.text: The goals of this law are to officially recognize that the federal government took the Reservation Swamp Lands and the Reservation Canal Lands without paying the Community for them. The law will provide payment to the Community for this taking of their land by the federal government. It will end all claims by the Community to the Reservation Swamp Lands and the Reservation Canal Lands. It will confirm that the current landowners who got this land in good faith truly own it. It will end all possible claims by the Community against the United States the state of Michigan and current landowners about who owns uses or lives on the Reservation Swamp Lands and the Reservation Canal Lands. It will give the Secretary of the Interior the power to pay the Community and take any other actions needed to carry out this law.

sections.title: Section 4 Definitions
sections.text: In this law these terms have these meanings. Community means the Keweenaw Bay Indian Community. County means Baraga County Michigan. Reservation means the L'Anse Indian Reservation located in specific township and range areas in Michigan including T fifty one N R thirty three W, T fifty one N R thirty two W, the east half of T fifty N R thirty three W, the west half of T fifty N R thirty two W, and the part of T fifty one N R thirty one W that lies west of Huron Bay. Reservation Canal Lands means the one thousand three hundred thirty three and up to two thousand seven hundred twenty acres of Community land located within the outside boundaries of the reservation that the federal government gave to the state under the law passed on August twenty sixth eighteen fifty two. Reservation Swamp Lands means the two thousand seven hundred forty three acres of land located within the outside boundaries of the reservation that the federal government gave to the state between eighteen ninety three and nineteen thirty seven under the Swamp Land Act passed on September twenty eighth eighteen fifty. Secretary means the Secretary of the Interior. State means the state of Michigan.

sections.title: Section 5 Payments
sections.text: As soon as possible after Congress provides the money allowed under this section the Secretary shall give thirty three million nine hundred thousand dollars to the Community. The Community may use this money for any legal purpose including government services economic development protecting natural resources and buying land. However the Community cannot use this money to buy land for gambling purposes. Congress allows thirty three million nine hundred thousand dollars to be provided to the Secretary for fiscal year two thousand twenty six to make this payment. This money will stay available until it is spent.

sections.title: Section 6 Extinguishment of Claims
sections.text: On the date when the Community receives the payment described in section five all claims by the Community to the Reservation Swamp Lands and the Reservation Canal Lands owned by people or groups other than the Community will end. On the date when the Community receives the payment described in section five the ownership rights of all current owners to the Reservation Swamp Lands and the Reservation Canal Lands will be cleared of all previous rights held by the Community and any of its members.

sections.title: Section 7 Effect
sections.text: Nothing in this law allows the Secretary to take land into trust for the Community for gambling purposes. It also does not allow the Community to use land bought with money received under this law for gambling purposes.
